references.title: Impact of ML-LA feedback system on learners’ academic performance, engagement and behavioral patterns in online collaborative learning environments: A lag sequential analysis and Markov chain approach

extracted.Features: [Couse module viewed, Course Module Completion, Assignment Form Viewed, Assignment Uploaded, Assignment Updated, Assignment Status Viewed, Discussion Viewed, Discussion Made, Personal Achievement, Group’s achievements viewed, Viewed list of groups, Viewed Group’s Profile]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data was collected and aggregated continuously. Study also includes separation between control group and a grooup that receives feedback at specific moments in time.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Intense Students: Students who exhibited, by the end of the course, the most activity when it comes to usage time, frequency of visits and completion of learning tasks.

Middle: Middle activity

Light: generally inactive
extracted.Implications:
It may be recommended to increase cognitively and behaviorally interesting instructions for students with low course engagement levels to move to the intense level.

Feedback mechanism needs improvement since it does not lead to better performance.
extracted.Comments:
Being willing to fully participate in the course was a mandatory requirement to participate in the study.

Differences between control group and experimental group are measured using ANCOVA tests.

No differences between groups on academic achievement or affective engagement.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size, with selection bias.

Ethical considerations between a group that receives feedback while another does not.

Undisclosed criteria for selection of clusters.

Specific to online contexts.

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was collected and aggregated continuously.

Study also includes separation between control group and a grooup that receives feedback at specific moments in time.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Combinations of transitions between 3 groups at 3 moments in time throughout the course duration:

e.g.: 
T1I→T2I→T3I → Intense on all 3 moments of measurement
extracted.Implications:
Engagement levels are likely to increase over time. 

Ability to transition from not engaged to an engaged state represents an important predictor of final performance. 

Learners who are light or transition to a Light state are more likely to need intense feedback.
extracted.Comments:
Being willing to fully participate in the course was a mandatory requirement to participate in the study.

Differences between control group and experimental group are measured using ANCOVA tests.

No differences between groups on academic achievement or affective engagement.
extracted.Limitations:
Small sample size, with selection bias.

Ethical considerations between a group that receives feedback while another does not.

Undisclosed criteria for selection of clusters.

Specific to online contexts.

Lag-Sequential Analysis may lose resolution in the context of a 14-week course being analyzed as whole and may benefit with its application on a smaller subset of events.

annotations.text: Giving effective feedback and monitoring its effects in an harduous task. Thus, in order to understand the effectiveness of feedback, the authors compare the behaviors and learning performance of two groups of students:

Samples uses 49 undergraduates. Differences in learning performance were assessed using t-test and ANCOVA. Diffferences in behavioral features were evaluated with lag sequential analysis and Hidden Marov Chains.

Results show that students who received feedback tended to be more engaged than the control group → e.g. they completed more elearning assignments. Moreover, the analysis showed that, while all students used all LMS systems, students in experimental group were more active in task and discussion views and posts, updates on tasks and group performance viewing. Moreover, all students tended to increase their engagement as the semester advanced, but the increase was more noticeable in the experimental group.
